bytes.topic: Database Selection — OLTP vs OLAP, relational vs document vs wide-column vs search
bytes.title: Cassandra's Place in the Database Landscape — Wide-Column, OLTP, and Where It Breaks
bytes.body:
Cassandra sits firmly in the wide-column category — not relational, not document, not a search engine. Its data model forces you to design tables around queries, not entities. Every table has a partition key (the distribution unit) and optional clustering columns (the sort order within a partition). There are no joins, no foreign keys, and no ad-hoc GROUP BY analytics.

This makes Cassandra excellent for high-cardinality, write-heavy OLTP patterns — IoT event streams, audit logs, user activity tracking — where access patterns are known upfront. TWCS (TimeWindowCompactionStrategy) is purpose-built for time-bounded data, grouping SSTables by time window for efficient TTL expiry.

But Cassandra is a poor fit for OLAP. It has no native aggregation pushdown, no columnar storage, and full-partition scans are expensive. For analytics, pair it with Spark via the DataStax Spark Connector, or export to a columnar store like ClickHouse. Lightweight transactions (IF NOT EXISTS over PAXOS) give limited OLTP-style guarantees but at steep latency cost — avoid them unless truly necessary. Know the shape of your reads before you model anything.
bytes.code_snippet:
-- Wide-column: design tables around queries, not entities
CREATE TABLE user_activity_by_day (
    user_id   UUID,
    day       DATE,
    event_time TIMESTAMP,
    event_type TEXT,
    metadata  MAP<TEXT, TEXT>,
    PRIMARY KEY ((user_id, day), event_time)
) WITH CLUSTERING ORDER BY (event_time DESC)
   AND compaction = {
    'class': 'TimeWindowCompactionStrategy',
    'compaction_window_unit': 'DAYS',
    'compaction_window_size': 1
   }
   AND default_time_to_live = 2592000;

bytes.topic: PostgreSQL — ACID transactions, indexes, JSONB, partitioning, extensions (pgvector)
bytes.title: Cassandra Dev's Field Guide to PostgreSQL — ACID, JSONB, Partitioning, and pgvector
bytes.body:
Cassandra's partition-first model makes PostgreSQL's feature set feel inverted. Where Cassandra defers consistency for availability, Postgres enforces ACID by default — every write is durable via WAL, with BEGIN/COMMIT blocks replacing Cassandra's lightweight transactions (LWT). Rollbacks are real; compensating writes are not needed.

Indexes in Postgres are global B-tree structures, unlike Cassandra's secondary indexes that scatter across nodes and trigger coordinator fan-out. Run EXPLAIN ANALYZE religiously; a seq scan on 10M rows hits the same way a Cassandra full-table scan does.

JSONB is Cassandra's map<text, text> on steroids: binary-stored, indexable via GIN, and queryable with -> and @> operators. You can enforce partial schema with check constraints — something Cassandra's schema-less maps cannot do.

Postgres PARTITION BY RANGE/LIST/HASH is a storage optimization on a single node — not a distribution strategy. Don't map it to Cassandra's partition key; they solve different problems.

pgvector adds vector(384) columns with <-> (L2), <=> (cosine), and <#> (inner product) operators. Index with ivfflat or hnsw for ANN search — semantic capabilities that Cassandra requires external tooling to reach.

bytes.topic: Cassandra — partition key design, wide rows, eventual consistency, data modeling for queries
bytes.title: Cassandra Partition Key Design — Model for Queries, Not Entities
bytes.body:
Cassandra's storage engine organizes data into partitions, and every read must target a specific partition key. Unlike relational databases, you design tables around access patterns first — the query defines the schema. A wide row stores all columns sharing the same partition key contiguously on disk, making range scans within a partition blazing fast. But oversized partitions (>100MB) cause GC pressure and compaction hotspots. Use a composite partition key like `(user_id, bucket)` to spread write load when cardinality is uneven.

Every write in Cassandra is a timestamped upsert — last-write-wins (LWW) via HLC. With `LOCAL_QUORUM`, you get strong consistency within a datacenter at the cost of latency; `ONE` maximizes availability but risks stale reads during node failure. Tombstones accumulate on deletes and slow down reads until compaction purges them — set `gc_grace_seconds` appropriately and avoid deleting from high-volume partitions. For time-series data, bucket by time period in the partition key to bound partition growth and enable TTL-driven expiry.
bytes.code_snippet:
-- Model: fetch a user's activity feed, newest first
CREATE TABLE activity_feed (
  user_id   UUID,
  bucket    INT,          -- e.g. YYYYMM to cap partition size
  event_ts  TIMEUUID,     -- type-1 UUID preserves insert order
  payload   TEXT,
  PRIMARY KEY ((user_id, bucket), event_ts)
) WITH CLUSTERING ORDER BY (event_ts DESC)
  AND default_time_to_live = 2592000  -- 30-day TTL
  AND compaction = {
    'class': 'TimeWindowCompactionStrategy',
    'compaction_window_unit': 'DAYS',
    'compaction_window_size': 1
  };

bytes.topic: DynamoDB — single table design, partition key + sort key, GSI/LSI, capacity modes
bytes.title: Cassandra vs DynamoDB — Single Table Design, Sort Keys, GSIs, and Capacity Modes Decoded
bytes.body:
DynamoDB's single-table design mirrors Cassandra's query-first modeling — both reject normalization to co-locate access patterns. DynamoDB's partition key hashes identically to Cassandra's partition key: same node-routing semantics, same hot-partition risks. The sort key is Cassandra's clustering column — it orders rows within a partition and enables `WHERE sk BETWEEN :a AND :b` range scans. Unlike Cassandra's multi-column clustering keys, DynamoDB restricts you to one sort key, forcing the overloaded-key pattern (`USER#123`, `POST#456`) to pack multiple entity types into one table — a pattern CQL avoids by simply creating separate tables per query.

DynamoDB LSIs extend the sort key on an alternate attribute within the same partition — conceptually close to a secondary clustering column. GSIs replicate data under a new partition key and sort key, making them analogous to Cassandra Materialized Views: both incur write amplification and eventual consistency lag.

Capacity modes are where the models diverge sharply. DynamoDB's provisioned RCU/WCU and on-demand billing have no CQL equivalent — Cassandra provisions physical nodes, not logical throughput tokens. When comparing costs, map DynamoDB read units to Cassandra's LOCAL_QUORUM read latency budget, not to any billing abstraction.
bytes.code_snippet:
-- DynamoDB single-table "Users + Posts" expressed in idiomatic Cassandra CQL

CREATE TABLE user_posts (
    user_id  UUID,
    created_at TIMESTAMP,   -- clustering col == DynamoDB sort key
    post_id  UUID,
    content  TEXT,
    PRIMARY KEY (user_id, created_at)
) WITH CLUSTERING ORDER BY (created_at DESC);

-- DynamoDB GSI on post_id  →  Cassandra Materialized View
CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW posts_by_id AS
    SELECT * FROM user_posts
    WHERE post_id IS NOT NULL
      AND user_id IS NOT NULL
      AND created_at IS NOT NULL
    PRIMARY KEY (post_id, user_id, created_at);

bytes.topic: Elasticsearch — inverted index, mapping, analyzers, query DSL, aggregations
bytes.title: Cassandra + Elasticsearch — Adding Full-Text Search to Your Cassandra Data Model
bytes.body:
Cassandra excels at high-throughput writes and partition-key lookups, but CQL has no full-text search. Pairing Cassandra with Elasticsearch is the standard pattern: Cassandra owns primary storage and write throughput; ES owns search.

ES stores data in an inverted index — each unique token maps to the documents containing it, the inverse of Cassandra's SSTable row-key structure. Defining an index mapping is analogous to a CQL CREATE TABLE, but field types control search behavior: keyword behaves like a Cassandra partition key (exact match only), while text fields are passed through an analyzer pipeline — tokenizer → character filters → token filters — at both index and query time. No CQL equivalent exists.

The Query DSL replaces CQL for search: bool queries compose must/should/filter/must_not clauses, giving AND/OR/NOT semantics without the ALLOW FILTERING penalty. Aggregations fill the GROUP BY gap — terms aggregations bucket counts across keyword fields while metric aggregations (avg, percentiles) operate on numerics.

Sync rows from Cassandra using the DataStax Kafka Connector or Debezium CDC → Kafka → Logstash. Treat ES as a read-only search replica; never use it as your source of truth.

bytes.topic: ClickHouse — columnar storage, materialized views, MergeTree engine, OLAP queries
bytes.title: Cassandra to ClickHouse — Trading LSM Writes for Columnar OLAP Speed
bytes.body:
Cassandra's write-optimized LSM-tree model excels at high-throughput ingestion but struggles with analytical aggregations — full-partition scans are expensive, and cross-partition queries require application-side scatter/gather. ClickHouse solves this with columnar MergeTree storage: only the columns touched by a query are read from disk, making GROUP BY and aggregations dramatically faster.

Cassandra developers will recognize the ORDER BY clause in MergeTree DDL — it functions like your partition key + clustering columns, controlling physical sort order and enabling sparse primary index lookups. Unlike Cassandra's SSTable compaction strategies (STCS/LCS/TWCS), MergeTree merges parts in the background and can apply transformation logic during merge via specialized engines like SummingMergeTree or AggregatingMergeTree.

Where Cassandra materialized views require careful capacity planning and lag under write pressure, ClickHouse materialized views are insert-triggered — they process data synchronously at ingest and persist pre-aggregated results into a separate MergeTree table. This eliminates the dual-write pattern Cassandra developers typically implement at the application layer to support analytical read paths.
bytes.code_snippet:
-- Cassandra: denormalized wide-column, write-optimized
-- PRIMARY KEY (user_id, event_time) controls partition + clustering

-- ClickHouse MergeTree equivalent — columnar, OLAP-optimized
CREATE TABLE events (
    user_id     UUID,
    event_time  DateTime,
    event_type  String,
    payload     String
) ENGINE = MergeTree()
ORDER BY (user_id, event_time);

-- Materialized view: pre-aggregate at ingest (no app-side dual-write needed)
CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW events_hourly_mv
ENGINE = SummingMergeTree()
ORDER BY (event_type, hour)
AS SELECT
    event_type,
    toStartOfHour(event_time) AS hour,
    count()                   AS cnt
FROM events
GROUP BY event_type, hour;

bytes.topic: TimescaleDB — hypertables, continuous aggregates, compression, time-series queries
bytes.title: Cassandra vs TimescaleDB — Hypertables, Continuous Aggregates, and What You'd Replace
bytes.body:
Cassandra developers typically hand-roll time-bucketing into partition keys (e.g., `(sensor_id, date)`) to avoid unbounded wide partitions. TimescaleDB automates this with hypertables — a PostgreSQL table transparently partitioned by a `time` column into configurable chunks. No manual bucket logic leaking into your schema design.

Where Cassandra forces you to maintain separate rollup tables — usually via Spark jobs or application-level writes — TimescaleDB offers continuous aggregates: incremental, auto-refreshed materialized views using `CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW ... WITH (timescaledb.continuous)`. Only newly ingested chunks trigger recomputation, not full scans.

Cassandra's SSTable-level compression (LZ4/Snappy) reduces storage but keeps row-oriented layout. TimescaleDB's `add_compression_policy()` converts older chunks to columnar storage, yielding 90–95% reduction on repetitive sensor data — compressed chunks remain fully queryable inline.

For range queries, `time_bucket('1 hour', recorded_at)` replaces your partition-key bucket logic with a SQL-native function. Pair it with a continuous aggregate and you skip full chunk scans entirely. The trade-off is real: Cassandra's raw write throughput at scale still wins. TimescaleDB earns its place when analytics and ingestion must coexist on the same dataset.
bytes.code_snippet:
-- Replaces manual (sensor_id, date) composite partition key
SELECT create_hypertable('sensor_readings', 'recorded_at',
  chunk_time_interval => INTERVAL '1 day');

-- Replaces Spark rollup jobs — incremental, auto-refreshed
CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW hourly_avg
WITH (timescaledb.continuous) AS
SELECT sensor_id,
       time_bucket('1 hour', recorded_at) AS bucket,
       AVG(value) AS avg_value
FROM sensor_readings
GROUP BY sensor_id, bucket;

-- Refresh policy (no external scheduler needed)
SELECT add_continuous_aggregate_policy('hourly_avg',
  start_offset      => INTERVAL '3 hours',
  end_offset        => INTERVAL '1 hour',
  schedule_interval => INTERVAL '1 hour');

-- Columnar compression on chunks older than 7 days (~90% reduction)
SELECT add_compression_policy('sensor_readings', INTERVAL '7 days');

bytes.topic: Indexing Strategy — B-tree vs hash vs GIN vs BRIN, covering indexes, partial indexes
bytes.title: Cassandra Indexing Strategy — Hashing, SAI, and When to Denormalize
bytes.body:
Cassandra's partition key uses Murmur3 hashing — equality-only, zero range support. Clustering columns are your B-tree analogue: sorted within a partition, enabling efficient range queries via CLUSTERING ORDER BY.

For non-primary-key lookups, legacy secondary indexes (2i) fan out to every node — avoid them on high-cardinality columns. SAI (Storage-Attached Indexing) is the modern replacement: compaction-aware, co-located with SSTables, supporting both equality and range predicates on arbitrary columns. Enable token-based text matching via SAI's analyzer config — Cassandra's closest equivalent to GIN. Bloom filters embedded in each SSTable act as a BRIN-style skip structure, letting reads bypass irrelevant data files without touching them.

There's no native partial index, but scoping a SAI query with a partition key predicate achieves similar selectivity at query time. For covering-index behavior, Cassandra still demands denormalization: model a second table whose primary key matches the query's access pattern. SAI reduces the frequency, but query-first schema design remains the primary performance lever.
bytes.code_snippet:
-- Partition key: Murmur3 hash → equality only
-- Clustering key: sorted → supports range queries
CREATE TABLE user_events (
  user_id   uuid,
  occurred  timestamp,
  category  text,
  PRIMARY KEY (user_id, occurred)
) WITH CLUSTERING ORDER BY (occurred DESC);

-- SAI: equality + range on non-PK column (replaces legacy 2i)
CREATE CUSTOM INDEX idx_category
  ON user_events (category)
  USING 'StorageAttachedIndex';

-- Scoped query: partition key + clustering range + SAI filter
SELECT * FROM user_events
WHERE user_id = ? AND occurred > ? AND category = ?;

bytes.topic: Query Optimization — EXPLAIN ANALYZE, slow query log, N+1, query planning
bytes.title: Cassandra Query Optimization — TRACING ON, Slow Query Logs, and Killing N+1s
bytes.body:
Cassandra has no EXPLAIN ANALYZE — your equivalent is `TRACING ON` in cqlsh. It dumps a full execution trace per coordinator and replica, exposing which SSTables were touched, tombstone counts, and wait times per stage. Run it against your CQL query, then look for high `sstables` values or tombstone warnings — both signal a partition that needs compaction or a TTL audit.

For persistent slow query logging, set `slow_query_log_timeout_in_ms: 500` in `cassandra.yaml`. Queries exceeding that threshold appear in `system.log` with full CQL text and latency. Pair with `nodetool tablehistograms <keyspace> <table>` to see read latency percentiles at the SSTable level.

The N+1 pattern in Cassandra is fetching a list of partition keys, then issuing individual `SELECT` statements per key in a loop. Fix it with a single `SELECT ... WHERE partition_key IN (?, ?, ?)` — Cassandra fans the request out to the correct replicas in parallel. Never use `ALLOW FILTERING` as a workaround; it forces a full cluster scan and bypasses the partition key entirely.

bytes.topic: Data Modeling — normalization vs denormalization, schema design for access patterns
bytes.title: Cassandra Data Modeling — Denormalize First, Query-First Always
bytes.body:
Cassandra has no JOINs. That's not a limitation — it's the contract. Schema design starts with your access patterns, not your entities. Every table is an answer to a specific query.

If you need to fetch a user's messages by thread AND by timestamp, you need two tables. Duplication is intentional. A `messages_by_thread` table partitions on `thread_id` with `created_at` as a clustering column. A `messages_by_user` table partitions on `user_id`. Same data, two write paths.

Partition key choice is critical. A poorly chosen key creates hotspots — all writes hitting one node. Composite partition keys like `(user_id, month)` prevent unbounded partition growth for time-series data.

Clustering columns control on-disk sort order and enable efficient range scans with `WHERE created_at > ? AND created_at < ?`. You can only filter on clustering columns in order — if your key is `(a, b, c)`, you can't skip `b` to filter on `c`.

Normalization belongs in RDBMS land. In Cassandra, every read should hit one partition. If it doesn't, your model is wrong.
bytes.code_snippet:
-- Two tables for two access patterns — no JOINs needed
CREATE TABLE messages_by_thread (
  thread_id uuid,
  created_at timestamp,
  message_id uuid,
  sender_id uuid,
  body text,
  PRIMARY KEY (thread_id, created_at, message_id)
) WITH CLUSTERING ORDER BY (created_at DESC);

CREATE TABLE messages_by_user (
  user_id uuid,
  month text,          -- composite partition to cap partition size
  created_at timestamp,
  thread_id uuid,
  message_id uuid,
  body text,
  PRIMARY KEY ((user_id, month), created_at, message_id)
) WITH CLUSTERING ORDER BY (created_at DESC);

bytes.topic: Transactions & ACID — isolation levels, optimistic vs pessimistic locking
bytes.title: Cassandra Lightweight Transactions — Optimistic Locking Without the RDBMS Tax
bytes.body:
Cassandra rejects traditional ACID in favor of tunable consistency — writes are atomic per-partition but lack cross-partition isolation. For conditional mutations, Lightweight Transactions (LWT) deliver compare-and-set semantics via Paxos. Under the hood, LWT runs a 4-phase protocol (prepare, promise, propose, commit), making it roughly 4× more expensive than a standard write.

LWT is Cassandra's form of optimistic locking: `IF version = 1` checks current state before applying the mutation. If the condition fails, Cassandra returns `[applied] = false` alongside the current row — your application inspects and retries. No lock is held between read and write; contention surfaces only at commit time.

For isolation, use `CONSISTENCY SERIAL` on reads that precede an LWT write to guarantee linearizability within a partition. `LOCAL_SERIAL` keeps the quorum datacenter-local. Critical caveat: LWT isolation is partition-scoped — there is no serializable isolation across partition keys. Multi-partition workflows require application-level sagas or distributed coordination (e.g., TTL-based lock tokens stored in a dedicated table).

Reserve LWT for genuine contention points: unique-constraint enforcement, inventory decrement, idempotent registration. Overusing it collapses Cassandra's write-throughput advantage entirely.
bytes.code_snippet:
-- Optimistic CAS update: only applies if current version matches
UPDATE users
SET email = 'new@example.com', version = 2
WHERE user_id = 123e4567-e89b-12d3-a456-426614174000
IF version = 1;
-- Returns: [applied]=true|false + current row on failure

-- Unique constraint enforcement via LWT
INSERT INTO usernames (username, user_id)
VALUES ('sravan', 123e4567-e89b-12d3-a456-426614174000)
IF NOT EXISTS;

-- Linearizable read before a conditional write
SELECT version FROM users
WHERE user_id = 123e4567-e89b-12d3-a456-426614174000
USING CONSISTENCY SERIAL;

bytes.topic: Replication — leader/follower, multi-leader, leaderless, consistency trade-offs
bytes.title: Cassandra Replication — Leaderless by Design and the Consistency Trade-offs That Follow
bytes.body:
Cassandra uses leaderless replication: every node is a peer, and any node can accept a write for any partition. The coordinator node — whichever node the client connects to — routes the write to all RF (replication factor) replicas in parallel. There's no single source of truth, which eliminates leader failover latency but introduces the consistency/availability dial you control per-query via consistency level (CL).

The core trade-off lives in the quorum math: with RF=3, QUORUM requires 2 nodes (RF/2+1). Set CL=QUORUM on both reads and writes and you guarantee strong consistency — any write acknowledged by 2 nodes will be seen by any read hitting 2 nodes. Drop to CL=ONE for writes and reads become eventually consistent; you'll occasionally read stale data until read repair or anti-entropy reconciles divergent replicas.

For multi-datacenter topologies, LOCAL_QUORUM is the sweet spot — it enforces quorum within the local DC only, avoiding cross-DC round-trip latency while retaining per-DC consistency. EACH_QUORUM forces quorum in every DC simultaneously, useful when you can't tolerate any DC serving stale reads but expensive under inter-DC partitions.

bytes.topic: Sharding — horizontal partitioning, shard key selection, hot spots
bytes.title: Cassandra Partitioning — Shard Key Design and Avoiding Hot Spots
bytes.body:
Cassandra's 'sharding' is consistent hashing: the Murmur3Partitioner maps every partition key to a token in the [-2^63, 2^63) range. Each node owns token ranges; a row always lands on the node responsible for its token. Vnodes (num_tokens, default 256 per node) subdivide those ranges automatically — no manual shard assignment needed.

The real danger is partition key design. Using event_date as a partition key funnels every today-write to one token, creating a hot node that degrades cluster-wide throughput. A composite key like (user_id, month) distributes load by user while capping per-partition row count. Keep partitions under 100 MB — beyond that, GC pressure and compaction latency climb sharply. Monitor with nodetool tablestats or query system.size_estimates to catch bloated partitions early.

For time-series, bucket explicitly: pre-compute a truncated time unit (hour, day) and embed it in the composite partition key. This bounds partition growth and ensures reads hit multiple nodes. Never use a monotonically increasing value (auto-increment ID, raw timestamp) as a standalone partition key — it serializes writes to a single token range indefinitely.
bytes.code_snippet:
-- BAD: single date key → all today's writes hit one token
CREATE TABLE events_bad (
  event_date date,
  event_id uuid,
  payload text,
  PRIMARY KEY (event_date, event_id)
);

-- GOOD: composite key spreads load, bounds partition size
CREATE TABLE events_good (
  user_id uuid,
  month text,
  event_id timeuuid,
  payload text,
  PRIMARY KEY ((user_id, month), event_id)
) WITH CLUSTERING ORDER BY (event_id DESC);

bytes.topic: Backup & Recovery — point-in-time recovery, backup strategies, RTO vs RPO
bytes.title: Cassandra Backup & Recovery — Snapshots, Incremental Backups, and PITR Without the Magic
bytes.body:
Cassandra's backup model centers on SSTable snapshots — hard-linked copies of flushed SSTables that are immune to ongoing compaction. Always call `nodetool flush` before snapshotting; skipping it leaves unflushed memtable writes out of scope.

For point-in-time recovery, enable `incremental_backups: true` in `cassandra.yaml`. After each memtable flush, Cassandra hard-links new SSTables into a per-table `backups/` subdirectory. Pair this with CommitLog archiving via `commitlog_archiving.properties` to replay mutations up to a precise timestamp — Cassandra's closest analog to WAL-based PITR.

RPO is governed by snapshot cadence combined with `commitlog_sync_period_in_ms` (default 10,000 ms). A 10 ms sync window bounds worst-case data loss to a single sync interval on a hard crash.

RTO depends on how much SSTable data `sstableloader` must stream back through the ring. In a multi-DC setup with `LOCAL_QUORUM`, you can restore one DC while the other stays live — dramatically cutting RTO without a full cluster outage.

Never restore by copying SSTables directly into the data directory on a live node. Use `sstableloader` so Cassandra handles token routing and replication factor correctly.
bytes.code_snippet:
# 1. Flush memtables — ensures all writes land in SSTables before snapshot
nodetool flush keyspace_name

# 2. Create a timestamped, named snapshot (hard-links; safe during compaction)
nodetool snapshot -t "snap_$(date +%Y%m%d_%H%M%S)" keyspace_name

# 3. Confirm snapshot paths and sizes
nodetool listsnapshots

# 4. Restore via sstableloader — respects token ranges and RF
sstableloader -d <seed_node_ip> \
  /var/lib/cassandra/data/keyspace_name/table_name-UUID/snapshots/snap_20240101_120000

# 5. Clear snapshot after offsite copy to reclaim disk
nodetool clearsnapshot -t "snap_20240101_120000" keyspace_name

bytes.topic: Connection Pooling — PgBouncer, connection limits, pool sizing
bytes.title: Cassandra Connection Pooling — Driver-Native Pool Sizing and Tuning
bytes.body:
Cassandra's drivers handle connection pooling natively — no PgBouncer equivalent exists or is needed. Each driver maintains a pool per host, bucketed by `HostDistance` (LOCAL vs REMOTE). Unlike PostgreSQL, where one backend process owns one connection, Cassandra's native protocol v3+ multiplexes requests over a single TCP connection using stream IDs — up to 32,768 concurrent streams per connection.

The three levers are `core_connections_per_host`, `max_connections_per_host`, and `max_requests_per_connection`. In practice, 1–2 LOCAL connections saturate most workloads; the driver auto-scales toward `max_connections_per_host` only when the pool is full. Oversizing hurts — every connection spawns a reader thread on the Cassandra node side, increasing GC pressure and contention on the `io_global_order` stage.

The default `max_requests_per_connection` is 1024. On low-latency intra-DC networks, raising it to 2048 improves throughput without adding connections. Keep `remote_connections_per_host` at 0 unless you're explicitly routing cross-DC fallback reads — unnecessary remote connections inflate coordinator latency and waste LOCAL thread budget on remote nodes.
bytes.code_snippet:
from cassandra.cluster import Cluster
from cassandra.policies import DCAwareRoundRobinPolicy
from cassandra.pool import HostDistance

cluster = Cluster(
    contact_points=['10.0.0.1'],
    load_balancing_policy=DCAwareRoundRobinPolicy(local_dc='datacenter1'),
)

cluster.set_core_connections_per_host(HostDistance.LOCAL, 1)
cluster.set_max_connections_per_host(HostDistance.LOCAL, 4)
cluster.set_max_requests_per_connection(HostDistance.LOCAL, 2048)
cluster.set_core_connections_per_host(HostDistance.REMOTE, 0)
cluster.set_max_connections_per_host(HostDistance.REMOTE, 0)

session = cluster.connect('my_keyspace')

bytes.topic: Security — encryption at rest/transit, row-level security, least privilege
bytes.title: Cassandra Security — Encryption, RBAC, and Least Privilege Done Right
bytes.body:
Cassandra's security model has three independent layers you must configure explicitly — none are on by default.

For encryption in transit, set `client_encryption_options` and `server_encryption_options` in `cassandra.yaml`, pointing to your keystore/truststore. Internode encryption (`server_encryption_options: internode_encryption: all`) is often skipped and leaves your cluster gossip exposed.

For encryption at rest, Cassandra relies on the JVM's `javax.crypto` via a `system_key` file or an external KMS integration (DataStax Enterprise adds transparent encryption; OSS requires filesystem-level encryption like LUKS or cloud-provider disk encryption).

For access control, flip `authenticator: PasswordAuthenticator` and `authorizer: CassandraAuthorizer` in `cassandra.yaml`, then restart. From there, use CQL roles with scoped GRANT statements. Avoid the `cassandra` superuser in production — create a dedicated admin role and rotate the default credentials immediately.

Cassandra has no native row-level security. Enforce data isolation at the keyspace or table level using role grants, and push row-filtering logic to the application layer.

bytes.topic: Migrations — zero-downtime migrations, backward compatibility, tooling
bytes.title: Cassandra Zero-Downtime Migrations — Additive-Only DDL and Schema Agreement
bytes.body:
Cassandra propagates DDL via gossip, not a centralized coordinator — schema state can temporarily diverge across nodes. For zero-downtime migrations, only additive changes are safe during rolling deploys: `ALTER TABLE ... ADD` is non-blocking, but `DROP COLUMN` leaves tombstone metadata for the full `gc_grace_seconds` window, and renaming non-primary-key columns isn't supported at all.

To change a column's type safely, use the dual-write pattern: add the new column, deploy code that writes to both, backfill historical rows via DSBulk or a Spark job, cut reads to the new column, then defer `DROP` until all nodes have compacted past `gc_grace_seconds`.

Before any DDL, verify cluster-wide schema agreement — `nodetool describecluster` reports `Schema versions`. More than one UUID means a node is lagging; applying further DDL during disagreement risks split-brain schema state that requires manual `nodetool resetlocalschema` to recover.

For version-controlled migrations, `cassandra-migrate` tracks applied CQL scripts with SHA checksums and exposes a `status` command to detect drift. Pair it with a pre-migration check against a local CCM cluster in CI to catch CQL syntax errors before they hit production.

bytes.topic: Monitoring — slow queries, connection counts, replication lag, storage growth
bytes.title: Cassandra Monitoring — Slow Queries, Hints, Connections, and Storage
bytes.body:
Cassandra exposes operational health through JMX metrics and `nodetool` — there is no built-in dashboard. For slow queries, set `slow_query_log_timeout_in_ms: 500` in `cassandra.yaml`; queries exceeding the threshold are written to `system.log`. For deeper inspection, use `nodetool settraceprobability 0.01` to sample 1% of requests into `system_traces.sessions` and `system_traces.events`, queryable via CQL.

Connection counts surface through JMX at `org.apache.cassandra.metrics:type=Client,name=connectedNativeClients`. Sustained counts approaching `native_transport_max_threads` indicate driver pool saturation.

Cassandra has no MySQL-style replication lag. The equivalent signal is hint accumulation: `nodetool tpstats` shows `HintedHandoffManager` pending tasks. A rising count means a replica is unreachable and writes are being queued for replay. If a node stays down past `max_hint_window_in_ms` (default 3 hours), hints are discarded and a full repair is required.

Storage growth is tracked per-table via `nodetool tablestats`. Watch `Live disk space used` and `SSTable count` — a climbing SSTable count with stalled compaction means I/O pressure. Cross-reference with `nodetool compactionstats` and tune `concurrent_compactors` accordingly.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Enable slow query logging — cassandra.yaml
# slow_query_log_timeout_in_ms: 500

# Sample 1% of queries into system_traces
nodetool settraceprobability 0.01

# Connected native transport clients
nodetool tpstats | grep -E "NativeTransport"

# Hint backlog — proxy for replication lag
nodetool tpstats | grep -A3 "HintedHandoff"

# Per-table disk usage and SSTable count
nodetool tablestats mykeyspace.mytable | grep -E "Live disk|SSTable count"

# Active compaction progress
nodetool compactionstats
